title: JWT "none" Algorithm Authentication Bypass
description: The application accepts JWTs using the "none" algorithm, allowing attackers to forge unsigned tokens and bypass authentication entirely. This enables unauthorized access to protected API endpoints as arbitrary users or with arbitrary claims.
steps: Create a JWT with the header alg set to "none" and a chosen payload, omit the signature, and send it in the Authorization header to protected endpoints. Verify that the API accepts the unsigned token and returns authenticated data.

title: Weak JWT Signing Secret Enables Token Forgery
description: The JWT signing key is weak and identified as "secret", allowing an attacker to generate valid signed tokens. This permits impersonation of users, arbitrary claim injection, and unauthorized access to protected functionality.
steps: Generate a JWT signed with the secret value "secret" containing arbitrary user and role claims. Send the forged token in requests to authenticated endpoints and confirm the server accepts it.

title: Empty Credential Authentication Bypass
description: The authentication flow permits login with empty credentials, resulting in a complete authentication bypass. An attacker can obtain authenticated access without providing valid username or password values.
steps: Submit the login request with empty username and password fields. Observe that the application issues a valid authenticated response or token despite the missing credentials.

title: Expired Token Acceptance
description: The backend accepts JWTs even when their expiration time is in the past. This allows continued use of stale or previously captured credentials beyond their intended lifetime.
steps: Use a JWT containing an exp timestamp that has already passed and send it to a protected endpoint. Confirm that the request is still authorized.

title: Missing JWT exp Claim Validation
description: The application does not require or validate the presence of the exp claim in JWTs. Tokens without any expiration can therefore remain valid indefinitely, increasing the impact of token forgery or leakage.
steps: Create or modify a JWT so it contains no exp claim and use it against an authenticated endpoint. Verify that the token is accepted without expiration enforcement.

title: Privilege Escalation via Role Manipulation
description: Authorization decisions rely on attacker-controllable JWT role claims. By modifying the role value in a forged or altered token, an attacker can gain elevated privileges and access restricted functionality.
steps: Forge or modify a JWT to set the role claim to a privileged value such as admin. Use the token against role-restricted endpoints and confirm elevated access is granted.

title: Client-Side Only Authentication Enforcement
description: Some access control is enforced only in the frontend rather than on the backend. An attacker can bypass UI restrictions and directly invoke backend functionality or protected application flows without proper server-side authorization checks.
steps: Ignore or bypass frontend checks and directly call backend endpoints using a browser, proxy, or curl. Confirm that functionality intended to require authentication or authorization is still accessible.

title: Client-Side localStorage Token Storage Exposure
description: Authentication tokens are stored in browser localStorage on the client side. This storage model increases the risk of token theft through malicious scripts, browser compromise, or cross-origin attack chains, and was explicitly identified as part of the JWT bypass to CORS to data exfiltration scenario.
steps: Authenticate to the application and inspect browser localStorage. Verify that the JWT or bearer token is stored there and can be read by client-side JavaScript.

title: UserID Type Confusion Enabling IDOR
description: A type confusion flaw in user ID handling enables insecure direct object reference behavior. By altering the type or representation of a user identifier, an attacker may access resources associated with other users.
steps: Modify user ID values or token claims using alternate types or representations and request user-linked resources. Confirm that data belonging to another user can be accessed.

title: SQLite Schema Information Disclosure via Verbose Errors
description: Verbose backend error messages disclose SQLite schema or query details. This information leakage helps attackers understand the database structure and refine further attacks.
steps: Send malformed input to database-backed endpoints to trigger an error. Observe that the response includes detailed SQLite error output or schema-related information.

title: IDOR on /payment/register-alias
description: The /payment/register-alias endpoint is vulnerable to insecure direct object reference. An attacker can register or manipulate payment aliases for another user due to insufficient authorization checks.
steps: Send requests to /payment/register-alias while modifying the user-related identifier or target context. Verify that aliases can be created or changed for another user's account.

title: Cross-User Enumeration on /payment/saved-recipients
description: The /payment/saved-recipients endpoint permits enumeration of saved recipient information across users. An attacker can retrieve or infer other users' recipient data without proper authorization.
steps: Request /payment/saved-recipients while altering identifiers or user context to target different accounts. Confirm that recipient information belonging to other users is disclosed.

title: Missing Negative Amount Validation on /payment/transfer
description: The /payment/transfer endpoint does not validate that transfer amounts are positive. This business logic flaw may allow invalid transfers or balance manipulation through negative-value transactions.
steps: Submit a transfer request to /payment/transfer with a negative amount. Verify that the backend accepts or incorrectly processes the request instead of rejecting it.

title: Wildcard CORS Misconfiguration
description: The backend uses an overly permissive CORS configuration that allows arbitrary origins to access resources. This can enable cross-origin reading of sensitive responses, especially when chained with token theft or weak client-side storage.
steps: Send requests with an arbitrary Origin header or load a browser-based proof of concept from an external origin. Confirm that the response includes permissive Access-Control-Allow-Origin behavior.

title: Development Artifacts Exposed
description: Development-related components and artifacts are exposed to users, increasing the attack surface and disclosing unnecessary implementation details. The report explicitly notes exposed development artifacts in the environment.
steps: Enumerate the application and inspect accessible frontend and backend resources. Verify that development artifacts or debug-facing resources are exposed in the deployed environment.

title: Exposed Webpack Dev Server
description: A Webpack development server is exposed in the target environment. Exposure of development infrastructure can reveal internal code structure, debugging interfaces, and implementation details not intended for production users.
steps: Probe the frontend environment for development server behavior and accessible dev-server endpoints. Confirm that a Webpack Dev Server is reachable and serving development-related resources.

title: Exposed WebSocket Endpoints
description: WebSocket endpoints used for development or application internals are exposed. These endpoints may disclose additional functionality, metadata, or channels not intended for unauthenticated external access.
steps: Enumerate the application for WebSocket connections and inspect accessible socket endpoints. Confirm that WebSocket endpoints are exposed and reachable from the tested environment.
